message:
feat(wal): add crash recovery metrics and tracing span wal_recovery (bd-14m.3)

Add WalRecoveryCounters with AtomicU64 counters: recovery_frames_total,
corruption_detected_total, frames_repaired_total, recovery_ops_total.
Add tracing span 'wal_recovery' with frames_replayed, corrupted_frames,
repaired_frames in NativeRecovery::finalize(). Upgrade unrecoverable
corruption logging from WARN to ERROR. 4 new tests.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-3plop.4","title":"Fault injection: lock contention storms","description":"Fault injection test for extreme lock contention scenarios. With page-level MVCC and SSI, lock contention manifests differently than in traditional databases — instead of deadlocks, we see SSI abort storms. This test validates that the system remains available under extreme contention.

Scenarios:
1. Single hot row: 100 threads all update the same row concurrently (maximum SSI conflict rate)
2. Hot page: 100 threads update different rows on the same page (page-level MVCC contention)
3. Lock convoy: one slow transaction holds a page while 100 fast transactions queue behind it
4. Abort storm: deliberate write-skew patterns that cause 90%+ of transactions to abort
5. Priority inversion: high-priority read transaction blocked by low-priority writer holding epoch pin

Metrics to capture:
- Abort rate (should be bounded, not 100% for scenarios 1-3)
- Throughput under contention (operations/second, should not go to zero)
- p99 latency under contention (should be bounded, not infinite)
- Livelock detection: if forward progress stalls for > 10 seconds, flag as livelock
- Memory usage: SSI conflict tracking under high abort rates

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 5 scenarios complete without deadlock or livelock
- [ ] Forward progress guaranteed (at least 1 transaction commits per second)
- [ ] Abort rate bounded and documented for each scenario
- [ ] p99 latency bounded and documented
- [ ] No memory leak from aborted transaction state
- [ ] Livelock detection mechanism works","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:27:47.022681729Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:44.382028259Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["concurrency","fault-injection","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.4","depends_on_id":"bd-2kkn4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.4","depends_on_id":"bd-3plop","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3plop.5","title":"E2E test: SSI serialization correctness under 100 concurrent writers","description":"End-to-end test that validates SSI serialization correctness under 100 concurrent writers with a serialization checker. This is the definitive proof that page-level MVCC + SSI provides true serializable isolation.

Test design:
1. Create a database with a known initial state (e.g., bank accounts with known balances, invariant: sum of all balances = constant)
2. Launch 100 writer threads, each executing random transfer transactions (read two accounts, check constraint, write two accounts)
3. Each transaction either commits or aborts (SSI may abort for write-skew prevention)
4. After all transactions complete, verify:
   a. Sum of all balances == initial sum (invariant preserved)
   b. No negative balances if constraint was always checked
   c. Committed transaction history forms a valid serializable schedule

Serialization checker:
- Record all committed transactions with their read-sets and write-sets
- Use a cycle-detection algorithm on the serialization graph
- If a cycle exists among committed transactions: SSI BUG — serialization anomaly was allowed
- The checker is a separate validation tool, not part of the database itself

Run configuration:
- 100 writers, 10000 transactions each
- Transaction types: transfer (read 2, write 2), deposit (read 1, write 1), balance check (read all)
- Mix: 70% transfer, 20% deposit, 10% balance check
- Duration: 60 seconds minimum

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 100 concurrent writers complete without deadlock
- [ ] Global invariant (sum of balances) preserved at all times
- [ ] Serialization checker finds ZERO cycles among committed transactions
- [ ] Abort rate < 20% (SSI is not overly conservative)
- [ ] Throughput > 1000 committed transactions/second
- [ ] Test runs in CI (reduced scale: 10 writers, 1000 transactions)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:00.562112726Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:43.462658238Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["e2e","mvcc","ssi","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.5","depends_on_id":"bd-2kkn4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.5","depends_on_id":"bd-3plop","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-3plop.6","title":"E2E test: RaptorQ self-healing with 5% random page corruption","description":"End-to-end test that validates RaptorQ self-healing by corrupting 5% of database pages randomly and verifying automatic repair. This is the showcase test for FrankenSQLite's core differentiator.

Test procedure:
1. Create database with known data (1000 rows across multiple tables, known checksums)
2. Run normal workload to populate WAL and trigger checkpoint (ensures repair symbols exist)
3. Close database cleanly
4. Corrupt 5% of pages: for each target page, flip random bits (1-8 bits per page)
5. Open database
6. Read all rows (this triggers corruption detection via BLAKE3 checksum mismatch)
7. Verify: all rows read correctly (RaptorQ repair transparent to reader)
8. Verify: evidence ledger contains repair records for each corrupted page
9. Verify: BLAKE3 witness proofs in evidence ledger are valid
10. Close and reopen database: corrupted pages should be permanently repaired (not just cached)

Corruption variants:
- Random bit flips (simulating cosmic rays / bit rot)
- Zero-fill (simulating sector failure)
- Random data overwrite (simulating stray writes)
- Header corruption (page header fields)

Expected results:
- 5% corruption with 50% repair symbol overhead: 100% repair success
- 10% corruption with 50% repair symbol overhead: should still succeed (margin)
- 20% corruption: may exceed repair capacity, test graceful degradation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 5% random corruption fully repaired (zero data loss)
- [ ] All corruption variants tested
- [ ] Evidence ledger entries for every repair
- [ ] BLAKE3 witness proofs valid
- [ ] Permanent repair (persisted, not just cached)
- [ ] Graceful degradation when repair capacity exceeded
- [ ] Test runs in CI (< 30 seconds)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:14.565093023Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:43.767831698Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["durability","e2e","raptorq","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.6","depends_on_id":"bd-3plop","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.6","depends_on_id":"bd-n0g4q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"bd-3plop.7","title":"Conformance: run SQLite TCL test harness","description":"Set up and run the SQLite TCL test harness against FrankenSQLite for compatibility conformance. SQLite ships with ~45000 test cases in TCL that define the expected behavior for SQL execution, error handling, edge cases, and pragma behavior.

Implementation:
1. Build FrankenSQLite as a shared library with the SQLite C API surface (sqlite3_open, sqlite3_exec, etc.)
2. Point the SQLite TCL test harness at the FrankenSQLite library
3. Run the test suite, categorize results: PASS, FAIL, SKIP (unsupported feature), CRASH

Expected status:
- Core SQL tests (select, insert, update, delete): target >95% pass rate
- Transaction tests (begin, commit, rollback, savepoint): target >90% pass rate
- Error handling tests: target >90% pass rate
- Extension tests (FTS, RTREE, JSON): SKIP (not yet implemented)
- WAL tests: many will FAIL due to different WAL architecture (expected and documented)

Tracking:
- Maintain a conformance matrix: test_name, status, notes
- Track conformance percentage over time (should monotonically increase)
- Investigate and document each FAIL: is it a bug or a deliberate divergence?

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TCL test harness running against FrankenSQLite library
- [ ] Conformance matrix with pass/fail/skip counts
- [ ] Core SQL conformance >95%
- [ ] Each failure categorized as bug or deliberate divergence
- [ ] CI integration: run weekly, alert on regression (pass count decreases)
- [ ] Roadmap for closing remaining failures","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:26.814471437Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:28:26.814471437Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3plop.7","depends_on_id":"bd-3plop","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:23:24Z"},{"id":2588,"issue_id":"bd-3ppz","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Task: TUI framework setup with frankentui and channel-based data feed

### What
Set up the TUI binary and core infrastructure using frankentui (at /dp/frankentui), with a channel-based architecture that allows background test threads to feed data to the UI.

### Architecture
```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Background Threads                               │
│  ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  │
│  │Benchmark │  │Corruption│  │  Correctness   │  │
│  │ Runner   │  │ Recovery │  │  Verification  │  │
│  └────┬─────┘  └────┬─────┘  └──────┬─────────┘  │
│       │              │               │            │
│       └──────────────┼───────────────┘            │
│                      │ mpsc channels               │
│                      ▼                            │
│            ┌─────────────────┐                    │
│            │  Event Loop     │                    │
│            │  (frankentui)   │                    │
│            └───────┬─────────┘                    │
│                    │                              │
│  ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────────────┐   │
│  │  Panel 1   │  Panel 2   │  Panel 3  │ P4  │   │
│  │ Benchmark  │ Recovery   │ Correct.  │ Sum │   │
│  └────────────┴────────────┴───────────┴─────┘   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Channel Messages
```rust
pub enum DashboardEvent {
    BenchmarkProgress { name: String, ops_per_sec: f64, elapsed: Duration },
    BenchmarkComplete { name: String, results: BenchmarkResults },
    CorruptionInjected { page: u32, pattern: String },
    RecoveryAttempt { group: u32, symbols_available: u32, needed: u32 },
    RecoverySuccess { page: u32, decode_proof: String },
    RecoveryFailure { page: u32, reason: String },
    CorrectnessCheck { workload: String, frank_hash: String, csqlite_hash: String, match_: bool },
    StatusMessage(String),
}
```

### frankentui Integration
- Review frankentui's widget system and layout primitives
- Use its app framework for event loop + terminal management
- Keyboard: Tab to switch panels, q to quit, r to run/restart
- Mouse support if frankentui provides it

### Headless Mode
When `--headless` flag is passed:
- Skip TUI initialization
- Still run all tests/benchmarks
- Output JSON results to stdout or file
- For CI environments without terminal

### Acceptance Criteria
- TUI binary compiles and launches
- Shows 4 panel layout (even if panels are placeholder text)
- Channel-based data feed works (send test events, verify display updates)
- Keyboard navigation between panels
- Headless mode outputs JSON
- Terminal resize handled gracefully

All 227 fsqlite-vdbe tests pass.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3pti","depends_on_id":"bd-35my","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:00Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":2590,"issue_id":"bd-3pti","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# 5C.4: Route SeekRowid/SeekGE/SeekLE/SeekGT/SeekLT through B-tree cursor

## What

Seek opcodes perform point lookups or range scans on B-trees. They're critical for WHERE
clause evaluation with indexes and for point queries by rowid.

## Opcodes

- **SeekRowid(cursor, jump_if_not_found, rowid_reg)**: Seek to exact rowid.
  → Maps to `BtCursor::table_move_to(rowid)` → SeekResult::Found or NotFound
- **SeekGE(cursor, jump_if_done, key_reg)**: Position at first row >= key.
  → Maps to `BtCursor::table_move_to(key)` then adjust if NotFound
- **SeekGT(cursor, jump_if_done, key_reg)**: Position at first row > key.
  → SeekGE then skip if exact match
- **SeekLE(cursor, jump_if_done, key_reg)**: Position at last row <= key.
  → table_move_to then prev() if beyond
- **SeekLT(cursor, jump_if_done, key_reg)**: Position at last row < key.
  → SeekLE then skip if exact match
- **NotExists(cursor, jump_if_not_found, rowid_reg)**: Like SeekRowid but different jump logic
- **Found/NotFound**: For index B-trees (key comparison, not rowid)

## Where to change

**Primary:** `crates/fsqlite-vdbe/src/engine.rs` — handlers for each seek opcode

## table_move_to semantics

BtCursor::table_move_to(rowid) performs binary search:
1. Start at root page
2. At interior pages: binary search cell keys to find child pointer
3. Descend to child
4. At leaf page: binary search cells for rowid
5. Return SeekResult::Found (cursor positioned on row) or SeekResult::NotFound (cursor
   positioned where row WOULD be, for range queries)

## Index seek (Found/NotFound/NoConflict)

For index B-trees, the key is a composite blob (not a simple rowid). The seek compares
using the record comparison function with collation awareness. These opcodes use
`BtCursor::move_to(key_blob)` instead of `table_move_to(rowid)`.

## Performance impact

Seek operations are the hot path for indexed queries:
- Point query: O(log N) page reads (B-tree height, typically 3-4 for millions of rows)
- Index range scan: O(log N) seek + O(K) sequential reads for K matching rows
- Full table scan fallback (no index): O(N) via Rewind+Next

Getting this right is essential for query performance.

## Tests

- `test_seek_rowid_found`: INSERT row, SeekRowid, verify Found
- `test_seek_rowid_not_found`: SeekRowid non-existent rowid, verify jump
- `test_seek_ge_range_scan`: INSERT 100 rows, SeekGE(50), Next loop → rows 50-100
- `test_seek_lt_range_scan`: INSERT 100 rows, SeekLT(50), Prev loop → rows 1-49
- `test_seek_on_empty_table`: All seek variants on empty table → appropriate jumps

 {"id":"bd-52a8k","title":"Seqlocks for rarely-changing config (§14.9)","description":"Implement sequence locks (seqlocks) for rarely-changing configuration data. Seqlocks provide wait-free reads for data that changes very infrequently. Readers retry if they detect a concurrent write (via sequence number check).

## Mathematical Foundation
A seqlock has a sequence counter (atomic u64). Writers: increment counter (odd = write in progress), write data, increment counter (even = write complete). Readers: read counter, read data, read counter again. If counters match and are even, read was consistent. Otherwise, retry.

Expected retry rate: if write frequency is F and read duration is D, retry probability is approximately 2*F*D. For config updates at 1/second and reads at 100ns: retry probability = 0.0000002 (negligible).

## Application in FrankenSQLite
- Database configuration (page size, cache size, journal mode) changes only on explicit PRAGMA
- Compile-time constants that may be overridden at runtime
- Read-heavy metadata where RCU memory overhead is not justified

## Alien CS Reference
Lameter 2005 Effective Synchronization on Linux/NUMA systems: seqlock analysis for NUMA architectures. Boehm 2012 Can Seqlocks Get Along with Programming Language Memory Models: formal analysis of seqlock correctness under C++11/Rust memory model (requires SeqCst for the sequence counter reads, Relaxed insufficient).

## Rust Implementation Guidance
- Define SeqLock<T: Copy> with sequence counter as AtomicU64
- Reader: use Ordering::Acquire for counter reads to establish happens-before
- Writer: use Ordering::Release for counter increments and data writes
- T must be Copy because readers read raw bytes without holding any lock
- For T larger than 64 bits: use compiler_fence(SeqCst) between data reads to prevent reordering
- Bounded retry: spin for MAX_RETRIES (default 1000), then fall back to mutex (prevents livelock under sustained writes)
- Implement as generic library in crate::sync::seqlock module

## Logging Requirements
- Log write operations at tracing::debug level: config_key, old_value, new_value
- Log reader retries at tracing::trace level (should be extremely rare)
- Log fallback to mutex at tracing::warn level (indicates sustained write contention)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Seqlock implementation (generic over T: Copy)
- [ ] Zero overhead reads when no concurrent write
- [ ] Retry loop with bounded spin count
- [ ] Integration for database configuration
- [ ] Benchmark: seqlock vs atomic vs mutex for config reads

 {"id":"bd-n0g4q.3","title":"RaptorQ decode/repair engine with BLAKE3 proofs","description":"Implement the RaptorQ decode/repair engine that detects corruption and automatically repairs pages using available repair symbols. This is the heart of self-healing durability.

Detection:
1. Each page carries a BLAKE3 checksum in its header
2. On page read, verify BLAKE3 checksum
3. On mismatch: page is corrupted, trigger repair

Repair:
1. Gather available symbols for the corrupted page (source symbols from surviving data + repair symbols from WAL/snapshot catalog)
2. If available symbols >= k (source block size): invoke RaptorQ decoder
3. Decoder uses inactivation decoding (O(k * log k)) to recover original source symbols
4. Reconstruct page from decoded source symbols
5. Verify reconstructed page against expected BLAKE3 checksum (from page header or parent page pointer)
6. If verification passes: write repaired page, log repair action to evidence ledger
7. If verification fails: escalate — data loss beyond repair capacity

BLAKE3 witness proofs:
- Before repair: hash of corrupted page
- After repair: hash of repaired page
- Expected hash: from metadata
- Witness triple (corrupted_hash, repaired_hash, expected_hash) logged to evidence ledger

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Corruption detection via BLAKE3 checksum on every page read
- [ ] Automatic repair using RaptorQ decode
- [ ] BLAKE3 witness proof for every repair action
- [ ] Handles: single-page corruption, multi-page corruption, contiguous range corruption
- [ ] Graceful degradation when repair capacity exceeded
- [ ] Unit test: corrupt 1, 5, 10, 20% of symbols, verify repair success/failure boundary
- [ ] Benchmark: decode latency for 4KB page (target < 1ms)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:19:51.022279049Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T18:20:42.173885645Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T18:20:42.173866149Z","close_reason":"Implemented unified repair engine with BLAKE3 corruption detection, RaptorQ-powered repair via InactivationDecoder, and RepairWitness proof triples. 15 tests pass covering checksums, single/multi/contiguous corruption, boundary percentages, batch repair, and compliance gate.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","durability","raptorq","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-n0g4q.3","depends_on_id":"bd-n0g4q","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-n0g4q.3","depends_on_id":"bd-n0g4q.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-n0g4q.3","depends_on_id":"bd-n0g4q.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-n0g4q.4","title":"Evidence ledger integration for RaptorQ repair actions","description":"Integrate the RaptorQ repair engine with the EvidenceLedger for full auditability of all repair actions. Every repair event must be recorded as a galaxy-brain card with:

1. Timestamp (monotonic + wall clock)
2. Page identifier (page_id, database file offset)
3. Corruption signature (BLAKE3 hash of corrupted data, bit error pattern if detectable)
4. Repair source (WAL repair symbols, snapshot repair symbols, or both)
5. Symbols used (how many source + repair symbols consumed out of available)
6. BLAKE3 witness triple (corrupted_hash, repaired_hash, expected_hash)
7. Repair latency (time from detection to verified repair)
8. Confidence level (repair symbols available / minimum required — higher = more confident)

The evidence ledger enables:
- Post-incident forensics (what was corrupted, when, how it was repaired)
- Durability health monitoring (frequency and severity of corruption events)
- Capacity planning (are we running low on repair headroom?)
- Regulatory compliance (provable data integrity chain)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Every repair action produces an evidence ledger entry
- [ ] Ledger entries contain all 8 fields listed above
- [ ] Ledger is append-only and tamper-evident (BLAKE3 chain)
- [ ] Query interface: list repairs by time range, page, severity

 {"id":"bd-n0g4q.5","title":"Async RaptorQ repair symbol generation off critical path","description":"Move RaptorQ repair symbol generation off the critical write path to ensure zero impact on write latency. Architecture:

1. Write path: page write -> WAL append -> fsync -> return to caller (NO encoding on this path)
2. Background thread(s): continuously process a queue of recently-written pages
3. Queue: lock-free SPSC or MPSC channel (crossbeam-channel)
4. Encoding thread: batch-encode pages (e.g., 64 pages per batch for amortized overhead)
5. Write encoded symbols to repair sidecar file
6. Backpressure: if encoding falls too far behind writes, optionally throttle writers (configurable policy)

Durability window: Between a page write and its repair symbol generation, the page has NO repair capability. This window should be bounded (configurable, default: 100ms or 1000 pages, whichever comes first). The window is documented as 'repair latency' in the durability model.

Thread management:
- Dedicated encoding thread (not from the query thread pool)
- CPU affinity hints for NUMA locality
- Graceful shutdown: drain queue before exit

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Write path latency unaffected by RaptorQ encoding (< 1% overhead)
- [ ] Background encoding thread processes queue continuously
- [ ] Backpressure policy prevents unbounded queue growth
- [ ] Repair latency window bounded and configurable
- [ ] Graceful shutdown drains pending pages
- [ ] Benchmark: write throughput with and without async encoding (target < 5% difference)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:20:15.125450220Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T18:54:08.348169212Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T18:54:08.348119770Z","close_reason":"Completed async RaptorQ repair generation pipeline hardening: verified off-critical-path enqueue, bounded backpressure behavior, graceful shutdown drain semantics, and commit-path overhead benchmark coverage (<1% in synthetic critical-path model).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","durability","performance","raptorq"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-n0g4q.5","depends_on_id":"bd-n0g4q","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-n0g4q.5","depends_on_id":"bd-n0g4q.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-n3p4","title":"Corruption Models: Define realistic corruption modes + safety rails","description":"## Goal
Define the corruption modes we will simulate and how to keep them safe/reproducible.

## Corruption Modes (Initial)
- **Bit flips**: flip N random bits/bytes (seeded).
- **Page zeroing**: overwrite a full page-aligned 4KB chunk with zeros.
- **Truncation**: truncate file by M bytes / to page boundary.
- **WAL tail loss** (if WAL present): truncate `-wal` after K frames.

## Safety Rails
- Operate only on `work/` copies.
- Always record seed + offsets mutated.
- Keep pre-corruption copy to compare.

## Acceptance
- Documented corruption spec and how each will be detected (checksums + sqlite integrity_check expectations).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:22:09.929714225Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:40:53.264120210Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T04:40:53.264100463Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of existing E2E suite tasks; superseded by canonical bd-z0xm subgraph.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["durability","e2e","repair"],"comments":[{"id":2887,"issue_id":"bd-n3p4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Acceptance Criteria

 {"id":"bd-ncivz","title":"Parallel WAL — Silo/Aether Style (§15.4)","description":"Durability scaling for concurrent writers via parallel logging. The current single WAL is a serialization bottleneck — all concurrent writers must serialize their WAL appends. Score 5.3 in the opportunity matrix. Critical for the concurrent-writer story that page-level MVCC enables.

## Background
SQLite's WAL uses a single-writer model (WAL_WRITE_LOCK). FrankenSQLite's page-level MVCC allows concurrent writers at the MVCC layer, but the WAL becomes the new bottleneck if it remains single-writer.

## Key Papers
- Tu, Zheng, Kohler, Liskov, Madden 2013: 'Speedy Transactions in Multicore In-Memory Databases' (Silo — epoch-based parallel logging)
- Johnson, Pandis, Stoica, Athanassoulis, Ailamaki 2010: 'Aether: A Scalable Approach to Logging' (consolidation-based group commit)

## Architecture
Silo-style parallel logging:
1. Per-core log buffers: each worker thread writes to its own log buffer (no contention)
2. Epoch-based ordering: global epoch advances periodically. All log records within an epoch are equivalent for ordering purposes.
3. Epoch boundary = durability boundary: at each epoch advance, all log buffers are flushed to persistent storage
4. Recovery: replay all log buffers in epoch order (within an epoch, order does not matter for committed transactions)

Aether-style group commit with consolidation:
1. Writers add their log records to a consolidation buffer
2. A designated 'flusher' thread (or the first writer in a group) performs the actual I/O
3. All writers in the group are notified when the flush completes
4. Amortizes fsync overhead across multiple transactions

Combined approach:
- Per-core buffers for zero-contention logging
- Periodic epoch advance triggers consolidation and group flush
- Single fsync per epoch covers all writers

## Expected Impact
- Write throughput scales linearly with cores (currently bottlenecked at 1 core)
- Group commit reduces fsync overhead (dominant cost for small transactions)
- Latency: individual transaction commit latency bounded by epoch interval (configurable, default 10ms)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Per-core WAL buffers with zero-contention writes
- [ ] Epoch-based ordering across buffers
- [ ] Group commit with consolidation
- [ ] Recovery correctly replays multi-buffer WAL
- [ ] Write throughput scales with cores
- [ ] Commit latency bounded by epoch interval
- [ ] Benchmark: parallel WAL vs single WAL throughput","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:22:55.572422603Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:32:41.507674312Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","durability","parallelism"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ncivz","depends_on_id":"bd-2kkn4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-ncivz.1","title":"Design per-core WAL buffer architecture","description":"Design the per-core WAL buffer architecture for parallel logging. Each worker thread has a dedicated log buffer that it writes to without any synchronization.

Design considerations:
1. Buffer allocation: pre-allocate per-core buffers at startup (size: configurable, default 4MB per core)
2. Buffer format: sequence of (txn_id, epoch, page_id, before_image, after_image) records
3. NUMA locality: allocate buffer memory on the same NUMA node as the core
4. Overflow handling: when a buffer is full before epoch advance, either block the writer or allocate overflow buffer
5. Thread-to-buffer mapping: use thread-local storage or core affinity to map threads to buffers
6. Buffer lifecycle: buffers are recycled after flush (double-buffering: write to buffer A while flushing buffer B)

Integration with MVCC:
- Each MVCC write generates a WAL record in the writer's per-core buffer
- The WAL record includes the MVCC version information (txn_id, begin_ts, end_ts)
- On commit, the transaction's WAL records are 'sealed' (marked as committed) in the buffer

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Design document with buffer format, allocation strategy, and lifecycle
- [ ] NUMA-aware allocation strategy documented
- [ ] Overflow handling policy specified
- [ ] Double-buffering scheme for concurrent write/flush
- [ ] Integration points with MVCC write path identified
- [ ] Prototype: per-core buffers with concurrent writers (no contention)","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:07.635963477Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T09:23:07.635963477Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["alien-concept","design","durability"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ncivz.1","depends_on_id":"bd-ncivz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:23:01Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
